content.en:
Domino Setup Theory

https://docs.google.com/document/d/1Z3O8BY1LbAMF6m55ufXtSdtBVwSJpMArtRrDT-p_CwM/

Rodney Kinney, April 2022

In a Fewest Moves Challenge (FMC), every sequence that leader to a Domino Reduction (DR) state can be broken into four stages:

<orient edges> <DR-4c4e> <trigger setup> <R/L trigger>

This document describes a theory for the trigger setup, which can consist of any moves that leave the cube in the DR-4c4e state. Any DR move will qualify, but R/L moves can also be used under certain conditions. Our goal is to be able to inspect any DR-4c4e state and estimate how many moves it will take to reach DR from that state, on both the normal and inverse scramble. We also describe how to find the optimal solution without resorting to trial and error and without memorizing any algorithm sets.

Move Estimation

The final state of the DR trigger setup has all of the mis-oriented edges and corners on the R/L face. For a short DR trigger setup, having pairs of mis-oriented edges and corners is good, but not all pairs are useful. A “double pair” consists of two pairs that form part of a 2x3 block. The long edge of each pair be aligned along a top/bottom face (a “top” double pair) or a side face (a “side” double pair)

A “top” double pair

A “side” double pair

Having solved corners plus one top double pair and one side double pair is both necessary and sufficient to bring all the mis-oriented edges and corners to the same face. The optimal trigger setup will form both double pairs while also solving the corners.

Let dt be the number of moves needed to form a top double pair, and ds be the number of moves needed to form a side double pair. It turns out that 2 + dt + ds is an accurate estimate of the moves needed to reach DR (including the final R/L move). The reason we can simply add the two numbers is that no single move can form both a top and a side pair; side pairs are formed only by U/D moves and top pairs are formed only by R/L/F/B moves. In my experiments, this estimate is often exactly correct and rarely off by more than a single move. The optimal trigger setup will interleave the moves required to form the two double pairs, but counting the moves needed to form each one independently is much quicker than finding the full solution.

There is an even easier approximation. Let pt/s be the number of top/side pairs already formed. Then dt/s ≈ 5 - 2pt/s. Therefore, a “lazy estimate” for the number of moves to reach DR is simply 12 - 2pt - 2ps. I find that this estimate is usually within two moves of being correct. Note that to have pt/t = 2, the pairs must not be interchangeable using DR moves, because such pairs cannot be used to form a double pair.

pt = 2

Both pairs count when

not DR-interchangeable

pt = 1

Only one pair counts when

DR-interchangeable

NISS Recognition

It’s common among FMC solvers to do a normal-inverse-scramble-switch (NISS) in order to see whether a DR-4c4e state has a short solution on the inverse scramble. To exhaustively check all of the DR-4c4e setups can be very time consuming. However, it’s rather easy to predict how many top/side pairs will be present on the inverse scramble, which means that a lot of time can be saved by skipping the NISS step unless the lazy estimate, 12 - 2pt - 2ps is low. If you’re looking to reach DR in ≤ 6 moves, then look for an inverse state with at least three pairs. Otherwise, you can save yourself the time of performing the NISS.

To predict top pairs on the inverse, look at the bad edges on the E slice. If there is also a bad corner showing the same two colors, neither of which is on the U/D face, then the inverse will have a top pair.

To predict side pairs on the inverse, look at the bad edges on the top/bottom faces. If there is also a bad corner showing the same color on the U/D face, then the inverse will have a side pair.

Inverse will have a top pair

Inverse will have a side pair

If the inverse has two pairs, they still must be non-DR-interchangeable to count as having ps/t = 2. This can be determined by comparing the colors shared between the two pairs. First count the number of colors shared between the two pairs: 0, 1, or 2. For top pairs, you need to also compare the non-U/D colors on the edges in the E slice to see if the colors are from the same slice (red/red, blue/green, etc.) or different slices (green/red, blue/orange, etc.). Having zero or two shared colors is good unless the edge colors are from different slices.

Number of shared colors between pairs

Edge colors on U/D face

pt = 2

pt = 1

Finding the shortest trigger setup

We follow the Hyper Parity philosophy of describing states up to equivalence under Half Turn Reduction (HTR). States that are separated by half moves or rotations are considered equivalent, and are described by a combination of a U/D orientation and a F/B orientation, where U/D orientation depends only on the stickers showing on the U/D faces, and F/B orientation depends only on the stickers showing on F/B faces. Parity is not relevant to DR setups.

We start with a full description of the theory behind Corner Orientation (CO), i.e. bringing just the corners into DR, and follow with a description of how to incorporate edges.

Corners

Orienting corners is a necessary step to reaching DR. This section describes a theory of Corner Orientation (CO) starting from a CO-4c setup state.

U/D Orientation

The U/D orientation is the configuration of non-U/D stickers on the U/D faces. With four mis-oriented corners, there are four possible UD orientations cases that are not HTR-equivalent.

Case

Equivalent to

Face

Slashes

Bars

Bar/Slash

When making a U turn, the U/D orientation changes depending on what stickers are showing on the U face.

Slashes

Face

Bars

Bar/Slash

Slashes

Face

Bars

Bar/Slash

F/B Orientation

The F/B orientation is just the number of U/D stickers on the F/B vs. R/L faces. There are three non-HTR-equivalent cases:

Case

Equivalent to

4-0

3-1

2-2

When making a U turn, the F/B orientation changes depending on what stickers are showing on the non-U/D sides of the U/D face.

4-0

3-1

2-2

4-0

3-1

2-2

Generators

There are only six different ways in which the two orientations can be combined, which means there are only six corner cases to recognize. These are the legal combinations with their generators.

State

Generator

Face-4

R

Bars-2

R U

Slashes

R F2 U

Bar/Slash

R U R2 U

Face-2

R F2 U R2 U

R U R2 U2 F2 U

Bars-4

R U R2 U F2 U

R F2 U R2 U’ F2 U

The CO-4c Maze

This diagram shows the possible combinations of U/D and F/B orientation, and the possible transitions between them. Solid lines indicate transitions as the result of a U/D move. Dashed lines indicate transitions as the result of an R/L move.

Slashes

Face

Bars

Bar/Slash

4-0

3-1

2-2

To find the optimal CO solution for your starting state:

  1. Locate your cube’s state in the maze and
  2. Choose a path to the Face-4 state
  3. Execute HTR moves to bring the desired U/D stickers to the U face so that the U turn will bring you to the next desired state in the path
  4. Execute a U/D move to advance to the next step in the path
  5. Repeat steps 3 and 4 until you reach the Face-4 state
  6. Execute HTR moves to bring the mis-oriented corners to the R/L face
  7. Execute an R/L move to orient the corners.

Edges

As you solve corners, you can intuitively choose setup moves (step 3 above) to form pairs along the way. For example, if an R2 move sets up the next desired state transition, then L2 will also work, as will F2-R2, B2-R2, F2-L2, and B2-L2. Select the setup that forms/preserves the most pairs and continue.

Example Solves

A scramble of the form <DR moves> R <DR moves> will generate a random DR-4c4e case. Let’s apply the theory to some solves.

L2 U2 R2 U L2 U' B2 R2 L2 U2 L2 U2 R F2 U' F2 R2 L2 F2 U2 B2 U F2 B2

Estimate: 7 (dt = 2, ds = 3)

Lazy estimate: 8 (pt = 1, ps = 1)

Solution:

// Starting state is Face-2

F2 B2 // top pair, setup to Slashes

D // Slashes, side pair

B2 D // Face-4

B2 L // finish (7)

NISS lazy estimate: 12 (pt = 0, ps = 0)

Inverse Solution:

// Starting state is Bar/Slash

(U2 R2) // setup to Bars-2

(D’) // Bars-2

(F2 U’) // Face-4

(L2 U L2 U2 L) // finish (10)

In this example, dt = 2 (F2 D forms a top double pair) and ds = 3 (B2 D2 B2 forms a side double pair). The estimate gives the correct move count; the lazy estimate is off by one. By inspecting the colors, we see that the inverse will have zero pairs, so we would know not to even bother checking the inverse, where the solution turns out to have 10 moves.

U2 F2 U2 R2 U2 R2 B2 F2 U2 L2 R2 B2 R U L2 R2 B2 F2 L2 U F2 U2 L2 U2 F2

Estimate: 6 (dt = 1, ds = 3)

Lazy estimate: 8 (pt = 1, ps = 1)

Solution:

// Starting state is Bar/Slash

L2 // top pairs, setup to Bars-2

D // Bars-2, side pair

B2 // setup to Face-4

U’ R // finish (5)

NISS lazy estimate: 6 (pt = 2, ps = 1):

Inverse Solution:

// Starting state is Face-4

D2 R2 B2 U2 B2 U2 // double pairs

L // finish (7)

This time, we have dt = 1 (L2) and ds = 3 (R2 U B2). The estimate is off by one, while the lazy estimate is off by three. Inspecting the colors, we see that the inverse will have one top pair (red-yellow-blue), and what looks like two side pairs (white-green-orange and blue-orange-yellow). However, the side pairs have one shared color, so they will be DR interchangeable and only count as one. The NISS lazy estimate turns out to be off by one when compared to the actual inverse solution, but our estimate would have told us that it was worth checking.

algs: L2 U2 R2 U L2 U' B2 R2 L2 U2 L2 U2 R F2 U' F2 R2 L2 F2 U2 B2 U F2 B2
